name: Niger Yellowcake
description: The Niger yellowcake controversy centers on claims made in the lead-up to the 2003 invasion of Iraq that Saddam Hussein's regime sought to purchase uranium ore concentrate ('yellowcake') from Niger. Proponents of the Iraq War, particularly within the Bush administration, cited this alleged transaction as evidence of Iraq's pursuit of nuclear weapons, thus justifying military intervention. The claim originated from intelligence reports, some of which were later revealed to be based on forged documents. These documents purportedly detailed meetings between Iraqi officials and Nigerien representatives discussing the sale of yellowcake. A key piece of evidence cited was the alleged existence of a contract between Iraq and Niger. However, investigations by the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) and independent journalists concluded that the documents were fabricated. The IAEA found the documents to be inauthentic due to numerous inconsistencies and errors, including a misspelled name of a Nigerien official and an outdated official seal. Mainstream scholarly and journalistic analysis overwhelmingly dismisses the Niger yellowcake claim as false and a deliberate manipulation of intelligence to justify the Iraq War. Critics point to the lack of credible evidence and the reliance on forged documents. The episode serves as a cautionary tale about the potential for politicization of intelligence and the dangers of using unsubstantiated claims to justify military action. The controversy significantly damaged the credibility of the Bush administration and raised serious questions about the intelligence-gathering and assessment processes leading up to the war.
subtitle: A pre-Iraq War intelligence controversy

claims.claim: Iraq attempted to purchase yellowcake uranium from Niger.
claims.evidence: Forged documents allegedly detailing meetings and a contract between Iraqi and Nigerien officials.
claims.criticism: The IAEA and independent investigators determined the documents were fabricated. No credible evidence supports the claim.

claims.claim: This purchase was intended to restart Iraq's nuclear weapons program.
claims.evidence: The claim was used as justification for the Iraq War, implying Iraq posed an imminent nuclear threat.
claims.criticism: Even if the purchase had been real, it would not have automatically indicated a nuclear weapons program. Iraq's existing nuclear facilities were under IAEA monitoring.

claims.claim: The Bush administration genuinely believed the intelligence regarding Niger yellowcake.
claims.evidence: Statements from administration officials citing the alleged purchase as a reason for war.
claims.criticism: Critics argue the administration either knowingly used false information or ignored warnings about the dubious nature of the intelligence.

keyFigures.name: George W. Bush
keyFigures.role: President of the United States, cited the alleged purchase.

keyFigures.name: Dick Cheney
keyFigures.role: Vice President of the United States, promoted the claim.

keyFigures.name: Joseph Wilson
keyFigures.role: Former U.S. diplomat who investigated and debunked the claim.

keyFigures.name: Mohamed ElBaradei
keyFigures.role: Director General of the IAEA, confirmed the documents were forgeries.

timeline.event: Reports of alleged Iraqi-Niger yellowcake deal begin circulating.
George W. Bush mentions the alleged purchase in his State of the Union address.
IAEA declares the Niger documents inauthentic.
Joseph Wilson publishes an op-ed debunking the claim.

sources.author: United States Senate Select Committee on Intelligence
